summary: Charity Majors argues that 'engineering-driven' culture often becomes 'engineering-supremacy,' where technical functions are valued above all others to the detriment of the business. She advocates for equal respect across all functions — engineering, product, design, sales, marketing — because business success is what ultimately enables a company to pursue its mission and values.
key_points: Engineering-driven companies fall into two traps: either alienating business functions or believing engineering skill transfers to all domains
Honeycomb had a zero-tolerance hiring policy against engineers who disrespected sales and marketing
Business success is the prerequisite for all other aspirations — without profitability, your destiny gets taken out of your hands
Cross-functional curiosity and respect can be fostered through deliberate cultural practices like deal write-ups, demo days, and recognition channels
Top-tier business talent is attracted to companies where they know they'll be respected as equals
money_quotes: If you can't make it profitable, your destiny will inevitably be taken out of your hands and given to someone else.
We DO NOT hire engineers who talk shit about sales and marketing.
So many companies who pride themselves on being 'engineering-driven' are actually what I would call more 'engineering-supremacist'.
Every function of the business is equally challenging, creative, and worthy of respect. None of us are here to peacock; our skill sets serve the primary business goals.
key_insight: True engineering excellence requires treating every business function as an equal partner, because without business success, even the best engineering culture becomes a cautionary tale.
